items.title: Announcing Ingress2Gateway 1.0: Your Path to Gateway API
items.summary:
<p>With the Ingress-NGINX <a href="https://kubernetes.io/blog/2025/11/11/ingress-nginx-retirement/">retirement</a> scheduled for March 2026, the Kubernetes networking landscape is at a turning point.
For most organizations, the question isn't whether to migrate to <a href="https://gatewa
items.reason: Ingress2Gateway 1.0 is a major change in Kubernetes networking and directly impacts cluster routing; the Wiki currently has no coverage of Gateway API migration.

items.title: Running Agents on Kubernetes with Agent Sandbox
items.summary:
<p>The landscape of artificial intelligence is undergoing a massive architectural shift. In the early days of generative AI, interacting with a model was often treated as a transient, stateless function call: a request that spun up, executed for perhaps 50 milliseconds, and terminated.</p>

items.reason: Running AI agents inside Kubernetes via Agent Sandbox introduces a new architectural pattern that is highly relevant for cloud‑native AI workloads.

items.title: Securing Production Debugging in Kubernetes
items.summary: <p>During production debugging, the fastest route is often broad access such as <code>cluster-admin</code> (a ClusterRole that grants administrator-level access), shared bastions/jump boxes, or long-lived SSH keys. It works in the moment, but it comes with two common problems: auditing beco
items.reason: Production debugging security best‑practices (cluster‑admin, bastion hosts, SSH keys) are a core security topic missing from the Wiki.

items.title: The Invisible Rewrite: Modernizing the Kubernetes Image Promoter
items.summary:
<p>Every container image you pull from <code>registry.k8s.io</code> got there through

promoter. It copies images from staging registries to

items.reason: The Kubernetes Image Promoter (kpromo) is a key piece of the supply‑chain; documentation on its modernization is absent.

items.title: Trivy supply chain compromise: What Docker Hub users should know
items.summary: We wanted to provide you information about a security incident that we became aware of that affects customers who use the Aqua Security Vulnerability scanner (Trivy) across multiple distribution channels including Docker Hub, GitHub, and npm. Between 18:24 UTC on March 19, 2026 and 01:36 UTC on Marc
items.reason: A security incident involving Trivy affects many Docker Hub users; the Wiki needs a dedicated incident‑response article.

items.title: From the Captain’s Chair: Naga Santhosh Reddy Vootukuri
items.summary: Docker Captains are leaders from the developer community that are both experts in their field and are passionate about sharing their Docker knowledge with others. “From the Captain’s Chair” is a blog series where we get a closer look at one Captain to learn more about them and their experiences.  To
items.reason: Interview‑style content about a Docker Captain is community‑focused and not essential for technical reference.

items.title: Achieving Test Reliability for Native E2E Testing: Beyond Fixing Broken Tests
items.summary: End-to-end (E2E) tests are particularly important for native applications that run on various platforms (Android/iOS), screen sizes, and OS versions. E2E testing picks up differences in behavior across this fragmented ecosystem. But keeping E2E tests reliable is often more challenging than writing t
items.reason: Test reliability for native E2E testing addresses a common pain point for mobile developers; no existing guidance in the Wiki.

items.title: How to Run Claude Code with Docker: Local Models, MCP Servers, and Secure Sandboxes
items.summary: Claude Code is quickly becoming a go-to AI coding assistant for developers and increasingly for non-developers who want to build with code. But to truly unlock its potential, it needs the right local infrastructure, tool access, and security boundaries. In this blog, we’ll show you how to run Claude
items.reason: Running Claude Code with Docker blends AI coding assistants with container security; a new how‑to guide is warranted.

items.title: Secure Agent Execution with NanoClaw and Docker Sandboxes
items.summary: Agents have enormous potential to power secure, personal AI assistants that automate complex tasks and workflows. Realizing that potential, however, requires strong isolation, a codebase that teams can easily inspect and understand, and clear control boundaries they can trust.  Today, NanoClaw, a li
items.reason: Secure agent execution using NanoClaw and Docker sandboxes introduces novel isolation techniques that merit documentation.

items.title: What’s coming to our GitHub Actions 2026 security roadmap
items.summary:
A look at GitHub Actions’ 2026 roadmap, outlining how secure defaults, policy controls, and CI/CD observability harden the software supply chain end to end.
The post What’s coming to our GitHub Actions 2026 security roadmap appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: GitHub Actions 2026 security roadmap outlines future defaults and policy controls; essential for CI/CD security reference.

items.title: A year of open source vulnerability trends: CVEs, advisories, and malware
items.summary:
Reviewed advisories hit a four-year low, malware advisories surged, and CNA publishing grew—here’s what changed and what it means for your triage and response. 
The post A year of open source vulnerability trends: CVEs, advisories, and malware appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: Annual open‑source vulnerability trends provide valuable data for security analysts; the Wiki lacks a summary of these trends.

items.title: Updates to GitHub Copilot interaction data usage policy
items.summary:
From April 24 onward, interaction data—specifically inputs, outputs, code snippets, and associated context—from Copilot Free, Pro, and Pro+ users will be used to train and improve our AI models unless they opt out.
The post Updates to GitHub Copilot interaction data usage policy appeared first on Th
items.reason: Changes to Copilot interaction‑data usage affect privacy and compliance; a policy‑impact article is needed.

items.title: Building AI-powered GitHub issue triage with the Copilot SDK
items.summary:
Learn how to integrate the Copilot SDK into a React Native app to generate AI-powered issue summaries, with production patterns for graceful degradation and caching.
The post Building AI-powered GitHub issue triage with the Copilot SDK appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: Building AI‑powered issue triage with the Copilot SDK is a concrete example of AI integration in DevOps workflows.

items.title: GitHub expands application security coverage with AI‑powered detections
items.summary:
CodeQL and AI‑powered detections work together in GitHub Code Security to identify vulnerabilities across more languages and frameworks.
The post GitHub expands application security coverage with AI‑powered detections appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: GitHub’s AI‑powered application security detections expand CodeQL coverage; documentation of this capability is missing.

items.title: oven-sh/bun bun-v1.3.11
items.summary:
To install Bun v1.3.11
```bash
curl -fsSL https://bun.sh/install | bash
# or you can use npm
# npm install -g bun
```

Windows:
```bash
powershell -c "irm bun.sh/install.ps1|iex"
```

To upgrade to Bun v1.3.11:
```bash
bun upgrade
```

### [Read Bun v1.3.11's release notes on Bun's
items.reason: Bun v1.3.11 release notes are routine binary upgrade instructions.
